summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews Bob Walsh's e-book 'Micro-ISV Sites that Sell!' despite his strong general dislike of e-books as a format. The book treats marketing concepts like Unique Selling Propositions (USPs) as engineering design patterns, making them accessible and systematic for software developers. McKenzie highlights how the book helped him recognize marketing improvements on his own Bingo Card Creator site, and praises Walsh's case-study approach. While noting some structural issues and arguing the $19 price is far too low for its value, McKenzie gives the book a strong recommendation as a practical marketing resource for software engineers.
key_points: Treating marketing as a design pattern — naming, studying, systemizing, and replicating — makes it accessible to engineers who would otherwise learn through painful trial and error
A USP must permeate every element of your site: tone, word choice, feature descriptions, and visual design should all reflect your target customer's worldview
The distinction between features and benefits is critical: features matter to developers, benefits matter to customers, and your site should optimize for the latter
Pricing a $19 book aimed at software business owners undervalues the product and attracts fence-sitters rather than serious practitioners who would pay much more
Writing in your customers' language improves both conversion and SEO simultaneously
money_quotes: Essentially every uISV starts out with a five page site which functions as a product brochure and can't sell their product worth garbage.

After you've named something, you can study it, and how it interacts with other named things. After you've studied it, you can systemize it. And after you've systemized it, you can replicate the success of your implementation of it.
Features are important to you, benefits are important to your customers, optimize accordingly.
One of the best ways of connecting with a given market is to speak as they speak, to share their worldview.

If your intuition about your market says that they will respond positively to your choice of language, and your market does not respond positively to your choice of language, then your intuition is wrong.

I don't think that price communicates that this is a serious resource for an owner of a software company, which is designed to make you thousands of dollars once you implement the very actionable suggestions.
Pricing for the folks who least benefit from what you have to offer is not sensible as a long term strategy.
key_insight: Marketing becomes tractable for engineers when treated as a design pattern — something you can name, study, systemize, and iteratively test rather than learn through years of painful trial and error.
